sentences.text: 1995 Interview with Olga Kovacs.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as you were hearing this cannon fire, what happened to the life in the factory and your work and your day and your-- and the conditions there?
Gradually, there were-- there was less work, and less raw material.
And then some time around April 1944, we heard some rumors that the plant will be evacuated further to the west, and they stopped taking us there.
However, they marched us every day to dig anti-tank trenches.
Under heavy guard, we were marched in the neighborhood, not too far from the camp.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somehow we were elated, because posters appeared, saying that it's either victory or Siberia.
And they said that all the little-- little brooks that they had in town, tiny little-- I mean, little piece of water, they called it brooks, and they had little bridges over it, wooden bridges.
I mean, tiny ones.
They-- they put mines under it.
They were all connected they say, so that they are not giving themselves up.
But what happened one day, we woke up, the guards were gone, the Commandant was gone with her very collaborating Jewish kapo by the name of Sala, S-A-L-A, who was a nasty collaborator.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She loved to smack people, even old people, older women, yelling and screaming, and just to-- just to show how-- how good she is with the-- with the SS.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They all disappeared.
And from one day to the other, the swastika banners were changed to white flags.
Then we figured that this is really the beginning of the end.
However, we did not dare to move out from the camp, not even for a free walk around the perimeter, because we were still afraid of the SS and what happened.
Just a few days before liberation-- I believe it was before liberation-- the Russians were not in there.
I don't remember they were in there yet.
A young woman was-- some-- one of the women was shot in the yard of the camp by the SS.
So the Russian army came and liberated the camp on the day of the Nazi surrender, which was on the 8th-- on the 8th of May, 1945.
We would have preferred the Americans, but you can't have the Americans all the time.

Sooner or later, we had to leave the camp, because the Russians-- I'm sure they meant well, but to this bunch of emaciated prisoners, who could barely walk, they gave one of those heavy Russian soups and they broke up the storage facilities of the camp and just everybody-- they let in everybody there.
And of course, people were grabbing whatever they could and started to eat.
Everybody was starving.
The meagerest.
At the beginning, compared to Auschwitz, it was-- it was acceptable.
Acceptable-- it's-- you have to take it in terms of-- of acceptable food in a camp, not acceptable food in normal life.
So that-- we-- we-- so one day, that the lower floor and the mezzanine.
Was filled ankle deep with human excrement.
I mean, you opened the door, and you couldn't go in.
So we knew at this moment, we had to-- we had to leave the camp.
So we went into one of the empty storage facilities.
But by that time, we also heard rumors that there are problems with the Russians and rapings do happen, do occur.
Nobody-- nobody had any-- any strong facts about this.
These were rumors, but they were enough for us.
So when the-- the-- the first time we heard this, we-- we just moved out from the camp.
Where did you go?
In one of the storage facilities, within the perimeter, but not where the quarters were.
It was a large area with a considerably large yard in the back.
I really couldn't tell you.
Probably over 1,000.
I don't know, because there were also Polish people, Polish women.
Yes.
The Poles were there already by the time we-- we-- we got there.
They were somewhat better off, because they did not go through Auschwitz.
They were-- they were displaced from their own hometown, through-- through ghettos, to Parschnitz, but they didn't know about Auschwitz.
We were the first one-- they-- certain people told us that we are the first ones who came from Auschwitz.
Yes.
Everybody was Jewish.
And Sala was Jewish, the kapo.

Then one day the-- the Frenchman said that we were to move out from here also-- he heard about the rumors regarding the Russians-- and that they would protect us and they will help us to find our way back to Hungary.
At that time, still we did not have the slightest idea that out of a family of 14 only the three of us are alive-- stayed alive.
We didn't know it, absolutely nothing.
And the Polish women didn't know about it, either.
That there were camps.
There was Treblinka, the-- the pits, the mass graves, the [INAUDIBLE].
Nobody knew anything.
No, because they-- they-- the Russians came, and one or two people opened the storage area, and then they sort of disappeared.
So they probably went through town, liberating-- there were a number of small camps in the-- in the vicinity.
But I don't remember ever having seen-- I-- I-- I saw Belgian prisoners walking around town, but I-- I don't remember ever talking to Russians.
Of course, I don't speak Russian, so I wouldn't-- couldn't be sort of a help myself.
But we wanted to come back to-- to Hungary, but we had to get organized, and we had to catch our breath and all that.
And then a Red Cross unit from New Zealand came to camp.
And they were looking for people they wanted to have as their interpreter.
So I volunteered.
But-- but I was so weak and-- and so terribly emaciated myself, that I couldn't keep up with them except for a few days.
And they also told us that they could make it-- they could-- they could facilitate our journey should we decide to go to New Zealand.
And then we thanked them profusely, but told them that we want to be reunited with the family.
Yes, a Red Cross unit from New Zealand.
And whether they did not want to tell us the truth about Auschwitz.
Because probably by-- by middle of May, the Allied had a good idea what happened in the East, I'm sure, and in the West, and in all the camps.
So we-- we were not apprised.
So finally, we set out to go back to Budapest.

To Budapest first because we were hoping that my uncle, with his family, who-- who was hiding with Christian papers, at least they would have had a chance or could have a chance to-- to be still around.
Because we didn't know what-- what-- what-- what was happening in Salgotarjan.
And then we found them, and we stayed there for a while.
And gradually, the truth came out.
And we found out that my Uncle [?
Bela, ?] brother of Mother and Auntie, was shot in the death march between Vienna and Budapest.
We don't even know where.
I don't even know when.
We don't know where.
Not-- not much before the liberation.
Probably between January and May of '45, but nobody was sure.
And then Aunt-- then my other-- other uncle, Mother's and Auntie's brother, [?
Gino, ?] he came back from Mauthausen with a tattoo mark on his left arm.
And he got spotted fever there, but somehow he-- he-- he made it back.
But when he realized that his parents, his wife, and his small daughter did not return and that his brother is still missing, he went into deep depression.
We just couldn't help him.
And then one day he told us that he's going out of-- out of town on business, and he'll be back the same day.
And a day passed, and he didn't come back.
And then he was still missing by next morning.
Then, with Mother and my aunt, we went to the police station.
We went to the-- to the naval guardhouses on the Danube, to the morgue in Budapest looking for him.
He was nowhere.
Nobody ever saw him, nobody ever heard of him.
They did not find any-- any floating corpses in the Danube they could not or did not identify.
And then subsequently, we found all his papers he left behind.
It must have been premeditated suicide.
Because these were the early stages of the Russian occupation.
You wouldn't have dared to go out without a bilingual Hungarian and Russian ID, and that's exactly what he did.
That was on August 28, 1945.
And you know, the deaths, they go on forever.
Every single month there is a death.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Mother and Auntie went back to Salgotarjan a little earlier because we-- we felt that I should-- as long as I am in Budapest-- I-- I should attend some-- some-- some classes.
And I started learning French, German, and English shorthand.
And it was for a summer course, number of months.
And then, before the year was out, I went back to Salgotarjan.
Of course, we did not get back to our apartments.
And by that time, my uncle started a business.
And mother joined him, and then I-- I went to work in the factory as well.
I did the billing.
In Salgotarjan, the old business.
Who was hidden in-- who was hiding in Budapest.
Yes, they are the ones we stayed with for a while.
The first notion came during the journey back from Parschnitz to Budapest.
The first time, they put us up in a kind of a stable, I remember, but at least it was not cold.
Because in Czechoslovakia, the mornings and the nights are still very cool in May.
And I remember one morning I woke up with a goat resting on my foot, but it kept me warm.
And then the second time, they put us up with some civilian people.
And there was another survivor.
I don't know where she was from.
I think she was Hungarian, but I don't know which camp she came through.
And she said that-- she was the first one who told us.
Of course, the first question-- your family, my family.
When-- when you meet somebody under the circumstances, it's the first thing-- which camp are you from?
How about your family?
And then she said that did you know that most of the men over 52 were sent to the [INAUDIBLE].
We didn't want to believe her.
And then gradually, probably around June, July, the whole picture, the whole tragedy was around us, all of us.
For a while, yes.
And then in '46, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we decided to immigrate to-- to-- to America.
And--
Because we didn't want to live there.
The family was gone.
Our closest family was gone, and we wanted to start a new life.
It-- it was too painful for all of us.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mother and my aunt had a distant relative here, who immigrated like, in the late-- in the early '20s or the late '10s as a young boy.
And he-- he made it in America, and he became very prosperous.
And he-- he worked all his life, and really, whatever he achieved, he achieved with his 10-- the American career.
And he sent us the affidavits.
But by the time we got the visa registration, the American visa registration numbers, they closed down the Iron Curtain.
And having just spent a year in a concentration camp, we were-- we felt we are not fit to spend some very uncertain months in a DP camp.
Although, everybody knew, just as we have, that a DP camp is a far cry from a concentration camp, but nevertheless, we-- we couldn't take it.
So we stayed behind.
During the uprising in Hungary in '56, we didn't make it.
I didn't want to leave Mother and Auntie there.
I didn't want to go alone.
And the three of us, we didn't make it.
So--
No, we didn't.
Yes, yes, because I didn't want to go alone.
I mean, I could have had the-- the opportunity to-- to-- to come alone after '45, and-- and I-- I just couldn't.
I just couldn't.
I just couldn't imagine my life without my mother and my aunt.
That's the truth.
Until '49.
Oh, there are so many of you who came back from the camp and things like that.
And we-- we gave certain beddings and certain-- certain things to Christians, who-- who offered to help us and hide it.
And most of them, with a few exceptions, after the war said, oh, Mrs.
Kovacs, I can't help you because the Russians took everything.
That was a stereotype answer, with a few exception in Hungary.
The Russians took it.
They took ours, too, but yours as well.
Again, this is beside the point.
Because again, material things mean nothing.
I'm not impressed by money.
I'm not impressed by [INAUDIBLE].
This is absolutely nothing.
What counts in life Is this-- the goodness and how you-- how you-- what's your perception about everything.
That's what counts in life.
Nothing else.
Absolutely nothing else.
You can be destitute and poor as a mouse, and you are a millionaire because of your good heart and because of the knowledge that you acquired in life.
The rest means nothing.
Next to the family, I have to wait.
Yes, Mother and my uncle and my cousin's husband, who were hiding in Budapest.
And then eventually, the communists took everything we had.

But luckily, my grandfather had a-- a summer house-- a villa-- villa, what they call it in-- in-- in Europe-- on the outskirts of town, which Mother and Auntie sold because, of course, unfortunately, they-- they became the heirs of whatever was left.
And we bought a co-op in Budapest for the money they got from the house.
And we moved to Budapest.
By that time, we, again, didn't have anything because the communists took it-- took it away.
That was in the-- they took away gradually.
In '48 part of it, and '49, then total nationalization.
No private enterprise.
Not at all.
Perhaps a doctor or two was able to function, but they did not like that, either.
I mean, the authorities didn't like it.
So we moved up to Budapest, and started to work and-- all of us.
I worked first as a secretary in an architect's bureau.
I did correspondence.
I made translation-- I did some translation as well, and started studying music.
And I always wanted to go to the opera, to work at the opera.
And as it turned out-- of course, I-- I-- I never would have had a chance because of the capitalistic background, not a member of the party, our class was declared enemy of the party.
You know, the-- the usual communist nonsense-- this terrible, asinine ideology they have.
But luckily-- you remember the people I stayed with while I was going to school at [INAUDIBLE]?
Margaret Fisher, the daughter of the lady where I was staying at, had a sister, Annie Fisher.
She became a world-renown pianist.
She used to come to America.
Well, she just died in Budapest a couple of weeks ago.
Annie Fisher's husband became the director of the opera house, Dr.
Aladar, Toth-- T-O-T-H.
And he told the people that I need Olga Kovacs because she speaks languages, and I need a secretary who does speak languages.
Because I have foreign correspondence and all that.
That's how I got to the opera house.
Otherwise, I would have never had the choice-- never a chance.
Never.
Because I was-- I was an enemy of the party.
Toth Aladar was a Gentile.
The Fishers were Jewish.
Toth Aladar couldn't have cared less whether I was a Jew or a Unitarian or a Moonie or whatever.
He wanted a secretary with three languages.
Period.
I meant, actually, the discrimination.
That you couldn't have gotten--
No, because of my-- my-- my-- my-- background, capitalist family, not a member of the party, and-- and-- and the communist nonsense, not-- not-- religious.
No, they couldn't care less.
My mother and my aunt went to work in a knitting factory.
Mother did bookkeeping.
And Auntie did finishing work.
I-- I started working-- I worked at the architect's bureau from '49 to '54.
And I started at the opera, I believe it was-- yes, I-- I'm sure.
I'm positive.
I started in June of '54, and I stayed there until we left, which was in November of 1961.
I did not start the new season.
I finished the season-- we went by season at the opera-- in August, and I did not resume my duties in September.
But they were very nice to me.
And they gave me a paper.
I could go to the university and-- and have my three exams.
And when you had your language exams, you got an additional pay, which was 30% after the high degree.
That-- that's how they-- they classified it.
You had medium, middle, and high marks.
And the high marks brought you a 30% increase in your salary per language.
So it wasn't bad.
I had a chance to go to the West.
There was always a feeling among us that we want to get out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother and my aunt had a cousin who survived the camp and eventually got to Holland and got married there.
And I went to visit them, and that's the first time I had contact with the West.
That was in '58.
And when I went home, we started talking about it in great detail.
And it took us a number of years to really decide what we want to do.
There were no possibilities to leave the country.
Between '49 and '56, absolutely nothing.
The Iron Curtain was rolled down.
You couldn't even escape, unless you wanted to risk your life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then there was a possibility in '56, which we-- we couldn't-- couldn't make.
And from '57 on, we started hearing rumors that if you have a close relative or you have some connection in Budapest you may, you may not be able to get a passport and immigrate.
You also had to get an exit permit from the communists.
So what happened, again, we got the affidavits.
However, you couldn't get a visa, of course, from America.

So through some legal maneuver and through lawyers, we got three Honduran visas sent to us on legitimate paper, with a letter of invitation from Tegucigalpa, mailed in Tegucigalpa, that relatives want us to come to them.
We needed all this for the Hungarians.
We knew that as soon as we get to Vienna we go to the HIAS and we have to take it from there.
So we left Hungary with the Honduran visas, and we went to Vienna.
